title: Constitution de la République d'Haïti (1987, amendée 2012)
title_en: Constitution of the Republic of Haiti (1987, amended 2012)

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 1
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Type of government envisioned
Haiti is an indivisible, sovereign, independent, free, democratic and unified Republic.
National capital

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 1
provisions.content: The city of Port-au-Prince is the capital and the seat of government. This seat may be moved elsewhere for reasons of force majeure.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 2
provisions.content:
The national colors shall be blue and red.
National flag

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 3
provisions.content:
The emblem of the Haitian Nation shall be a flag with the following description:
two (2) equal sized horizontal bands: a blue one on top and a red one underneath;
the coat of arms of the Republic are: a Palette surmounted by the liberty cap, and under the palms a trophy with the legend: In Union there is Strength.
Reference to fraternity/solidarity, National motto

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 4
provisions.content:
The national motto is: Liberty; Equality, Fraternity.
National anthem

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 4-1
provisions.content:
The national anthem shall be the “Dessalinienne.”
Official or national languages

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 5
provisions.content: All Haitians are united by a common language: Creole. Creole and French are the official languages of the Republic.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 6
provisions.content: The monetary unit shall be the gourde, which is divided into centimes.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 7
provisions.content: The cult of the personality is categorically forbidden. Effigies and names of living personages may not appear on the currency, stamps, seals, public buildings, streets or works of art.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Republic of Haiti
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 7-1
provisions.content: Use of effigies of deceased persons must be approved by the Legislature.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 8

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 8-1
provisions.content:
The territory of the Haitian Republic is inviolable and may not be alienated either in whole or in part by any treaty or convention.
Municipal government, Subsidiary unit government

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 9
provisions.content:
The territory of the Republic is divided and subdivided into Departments, Arrondissements, Communes, Quartiers and Communal Sections.
Subsidiary unit government, Municipal government

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.section: TITLE I. The Republic of Haiti; Its Emblem and Its Symbols
provisions.title: Article 9-1
provisions.content: The law determines the number and boundaries of these divisions and subdivisions, and regulates their organization and operation.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 10
provisions.content:
The regulations governing Haitian nationality shall be determined by law.
Right to renounce citizenship, Requirements for birthright citizenship

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 11
provisions.content: Any person born of a Haitian father or Haitian mother who are themselves native-born Haitians and have never renounced their nationality possesses Haitian nationality at the time of birth.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 11-1
provisions.content:
[Inserted by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Requirements for naturalization
The law establishes the conditions in which an individual may acquire the Haitian nationality.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 12
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Any Haitian, except for the privileges reserved to Haitians of origin, is subject to all the rights, duties and obligations attached to their Haitian nationality.
No Haitian can make their foreign nationality prevail on the territory of the Republic.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 12-1
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 12-2
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 13
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 14
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. Territory of the Haitian Republic
provisions.title: Article 15
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Nature of the Citizenship
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 16
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Conditions for revoking citizenship
The enjoyment, and the exercise of the civil and political rights constitute the quality of the citizen. The suspension or the loss of these rights is regulated by the law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Nature of the Citizenship
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 16-1
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Nature of the Citizenship
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 16-2
provisions.content:
The age of majority is eighteen (18) years.
Restrictions on voting

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Nature of the Citizenship
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 17
provisions.content: All Haitians, regardless of sex or marital status, who have attained twenty-one years of age may exercise their political and civil rights if the meet the other conditions prescribed by the Constitution and by law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Nature of the Citizenship
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 17-1
provisions.content:
[Inserted by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
First chamber representation quotas, Second chamber representation quotas
The principle of the quota of at least thirty percent (30%) of women is recognized at all levels of national life, notably in the public services.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. The Nature of the Citizenship
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 18
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
General guarantee of equality, Right to renounce citizenship
Haitians shall be equal before the law, subject to the special advantages conferred on native-born Haitians who have never renounced their nationality.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Right to Life and Health
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 19
provisions.content:
The State has the absolute obligation to guarantee the right to life, health, and respect of the human person for all citizens without distinction, in conformity with the Universal Declaration of the Rights of Man.
Prohibition of capital punishment

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Right to Life and Health
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 20
provisions.content: The death penalty is abolished in all cases.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Right to Life and Health
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 21
provisions.content: The crime of high treason consists in bearing arms in a foreign army against the Republic, serving a foreign nation in a conflict with the Republic, in any official’s stealing state property, entrusted to his management, or any violation of the Constitution by those responsible for enforcing it.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Right to Life and Health
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 21-1
provisions.content:
The crime of high treason is punishable by forced labor for life without commutation of sentence.
Right to shelter

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Right to Life and Health
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 22
provisions.content: The State recognizes the right of every citizen to decent housing, education, food and social security.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Right to Life and Health
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 23
provisions.content: The State has the obligation to ensure for all citizens in all territorial divisions appropriate means to ensure protection, maintenance and restoration of their health by establishing hospitals, health centers and dispensaries.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 24
provisions.content:
Individual liberty is guaranteed and protected by the State.
Principle of no punishment without law

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 24-1
provisions.content:
No one may be prosecuted, arrested or detained except in the cases determined by law and in the manner it prescribes.
Protection from unjustified restraint

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 24-2
provisions.content:
Except where the perpetrator of a crime is caught in the act, no one may be arrested or detained other than by written order of a legally competent official.
Protection from unjustified restraint

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 24-3
provisions.content:
For such an order to be carried out, the following requirements must be met:
It must formally state the reason in Creole and in French for the arrest or detention and the provision of the law that provides for punishment of the act charged.
Legal notice must be given and a copy of the order must be left with the accused at the time of its execution;
The accursed must be notified of his right to be assisted by counsel at all phases of the investigation of the case up to the final judgment;
Except where the perpetrator of a crime is caught in the act, no arrest by warrant and no search may take place between six (6) p.m. and six (6) a.m.
Responsibility for an offense is personal, and no one may be arrested in the place of another.
Prohibition of cruel treatment, Prohibition of torture

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 25
provisions.content:
Any unnecessary force or restraint in the apprehension of a person or in keeping him under arrest, or any psychological pressure or physical brutality, especially during interrogation, is forbidden.
Right to counsel

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 25-1
provisions.content: No one may be interrogated without his attorney or a witness of his choice being present.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 26
provisions.content:
No one may be kept under arrest more than forty-eight (48) hours unless he has appeared before a judge asked to rule on the legality of the arrest and the judge has confirmed the arrest by a well-founded decision.
Right to appeal judicial decisions

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 26-1
provisions.content:
In the case of a petty violation, the accursed shall be referred to a justice of the peace, who shall then hand down a final decision.
In the case for more serious offenses or crimes, an appeal may be filed, without prior permission, simply by addressing a petition to the presiding judge of the competent civil court, who, on the basis of the oral statement of the prosecutor, shall rule on the legality of the arrest and detention, in a special session of the court, without postponement or rotation of judges, all other cases being suspended.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 26-2
provisions.content: If the arrest is judged to be illegal, the judge shall order the immediate release of the arrested person and that order shall be enforceable immediately, regardless of any appeal to a higher court or the supreme court for an order forbidding enforcement of the judgment.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 27
provisions.content: Any violation of the provisions on individual liberty are arbitrary acts. Injured parties may, without prior authorization, appeal to the competent courts, to bring suit against the authors and perpetrators of these arbitrary acts, regardless of their rank or the body to which they belong.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Individual Liberty
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 27-1
provisions.content: Government officials and employees are directly liable under civil and administrative criminal law for acts carried out in violation of rights. In such cases, civil liability extends to the State as well.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. Freedom of Expression
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 28
provisions.content:
Every Haitian has the right to express his opinions freely on any matter by any means he chooses.
Freedom of press

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. Freedom of Expression
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 28-1
provisions.content: Journalists shall freely exercise their profession within the framework of the law. Such exercise may not be subject to any authorization or censorship, except in the case of war.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. Freedom of Expression
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 28-2
provisions.content: Journalists may not be compelled to reveal their sources. However, it is their duty to verify the authenticity and accuracy of information. It is also this obligation to respect the ethics of their profession.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. Freedom of Expression
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 28-3
provisions.content:
All offenses involving the press and abuses of the right of expression come under the code of criminal law.
Right of petition

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. Freedom of Expression
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 29
provisions.content: The right of petition is recognized. It is exercised personally by one or more citizens but never in the name of a body.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. Freedom of Expression
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 29-1
provisions.content:
[Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Freedom of opinion/thought/conscience, Freedom of religion

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Freedom of Conscience
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 30
provisions.content: All religions and faiths shall be freely exercised. Everyone is entitled to profess his religion and practice his faith, provided the exercise of that right does not disturb law and order.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Freedom of Conscience
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 30-1
provisions.content: No one may be compelled to belong to a religious organization or to follow a religious teaching contrary to his convictions.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Freedom of Conscience
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 30-2
provisions.content: The law establishes the conditions for recognition and practice of religions and faiths.

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Freedom of Assembly and Association
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 31
provisions.content:
Freedom of unarmed assembly and association for political, economic, social, cultural or any other peaceful purposes is guaranteed.
Restrictions on political parties, Right to form political parties

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Freedom of Assembly and Association
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 31-1
provisions.content: Political parties and groups shall compete with each other in the exercise of suffrage. They may be established and may carry out their activities freely. They must respect the principles of national and democratic sovereignty. The law determines the conditions for their recognition and operation, and the advantages and privileges reserved to them.

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Freedom of Assembly and Association
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 31-1
provisions.content:
[Inserted by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Restrictions on political parties
Any law concerning the Political Parties must reserve in its structures and in its mechanisms of functioning a treatment in conformity with the principle of the quota of at least thirty percent (30%) of women as expressed in Article 17-1.

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Freedom of Assembly and Association
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 31-2
provisions.content: The police authorities must be notified in advance of assemblies outdoors in public places.

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Freedom of Assembly and Association
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 31-3
provisions.content: No one may be compelled to join any association of any kind.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Right to academic freedom
The State guarantees the right to education. Instruction is free to all the degrees. This freedom is exercised under the control of the State.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-1
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Free education
Education is a responsibility of the State and of the territorial collectivities. They must place school freely within the reach of all, and see to the level of training of the teachers of the public and non-public sectors.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-2
provisions.content: The first responsibility of the State and its territorial divisions is education of the masses, which is the only way the country can be developed. The State shall encourage and facilitate private enterprise in this field.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-3
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Compulsory education
Fundamental education is obligatory. The classical necessities and didactic materials shall be placed freely by the State at the disposition of the students at the level of fundamental education.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-4
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Agricultural, vocational and technical education is a responsibility taken by the State and the territorial collectivities.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-5
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Preschool and maternal instruction will be a responsibility taken by the State and the territorial collectivities.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-6
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Access to higher education
The access to superior studies is open, in full equality, to all.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-7
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The State must see to it that each territorial collectivity is endowed with establishments adapted to the needs of its development.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-8
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
State support for the disabled
The State guarantees to persons with special needs the protection, the education and any other means necessary for their full enjoyment and for their integration or reintegration into society.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-9
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The State and the territorial collectivities have as a duty to make all the provisions necessary with a view to intensify the campaign of literacy of the masses. They encourage all private initiatives directed to this end.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 32-10
provisions.content:
Teachers are entitled to a fair salary.
Right to academic freedom

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 33
provisions.content: There shall be freedom of education at all levels. This freedom shall be exercised under the control of the State.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 34
provisions.content: Except where perpetrators of crimes are caught in the act, the premises of educational establishments are inviolable. No police forces may enter them except with the permission of the supervisors of those establishments.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Education and Teaching
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 34-1
provisions.content: This provision does not apply when an educational establishment is used for the purposes.

provisions.chapter: SECTION G. Freedom to Work
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 35
provisions.content: Freedom to work is guaranteed; every citizen has the obligation to engage in work of his choice to meet his own and his family’s needs, and to cooperate with the State in the establishment of a social security system.

provisions.chapter: SECTION G. Freedom to Work
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 35-1
provisions.content:
Every employee of a private or public institution is entitled to a fair wage, to rest, to a paid annual vacation and to a bonus.
Equality regardless of gender, Right to just remuneration, Equality regardless of creed or belief

provisions.chapter: SECTION G. Freedom to Work
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 35-2
provisions.content:
The State guarantees workers equal working conditions and wages regardless of their sex, beliefs, opinions and marital status.
Right to join trade unions

provisions.chapter: SECTION G. Freedom to Work
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 35-3
provisions.content: Trade union freedom is guaranteed. any worker in the public or private sector may join a union representing his particular occupation solely to protect his work interests.

provisions.chapter: SECTION G. Freedom to Work
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 35-4
provisions.content:
Unions are essentially nonpolitical, nonprofit, and nondenominational. No one may be forced to join a union.
Right to strike

provisions.chapter: SECTION G. Freedom to Work
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 35-5
provisions.content:
The right to strike is recognized under the limits set by law.
Limits on employment of children

provisions.chapter: SECTION G. Freedom to Work
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 35-6
provisions.content: The minimum age for gainful employment is set by law. Special laws govern the work of minors and servants.

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 36
provisions.content:
Private property is recognized and guaranteed. The law specifies the manner of acquiring and enjoying it, and the limits placed upon it.
Protection from expropriation

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 36-1
provisions.content:
Expropriation for a public purpose may be effected only by payment or deposit ordered by a court in favor of the person entitled thereto, of fair compensation established in advance by an expert evaluation.
If the initial project is abandoned, the expropriation is canceled. The property may not be subject to any speculation and must be restored to its original owner without any reimbursement for the small holder. The expropriation measure is effective upon the startup of the project.
Protection from expropriation

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 36-2
provisions.content:
Nationalization and confiscation of goods, property and buildings for political reasons are forbidden.
No one may be deprived of his legitimate right of ownership other than by a final judgment by a court of ordinary law, except under an agrarian reform.

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 36-3
provisions.content: Ownership also entails obligations. Uses of property cannot be contrary to the general interest.

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 36-4
provisions.content:
Landowners must cultivate, work, and protect their land, particularly against erosion. The penalty for failure to fulfill this obligation shall be prescribed by law.
Ownership of natural resources

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 36-5
provisions.content: The right to own property does not extend to the coasts, springs, rivers, water courses, mines and quarries. They are part of the State’s public domain.

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 36-6
provisions.content: The law shall establish regulations governing freedom to prospect for and work mines, or bearing earths, and quarries, ensuring an equal share of the profits of such exploitation to the owner of the land and to the Haitian State or its concessionnaires.

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 37
provisions.content:
The law shall set conditions for land division and aggregation in terms of a territorial management plan and the well-being of the communities concerned, within the framework of agrarian reform.
Provisions for intellectual property, Reference to science, Reference to art

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 38
provisions.content: Scientific, literary and artistic property is protected by law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION H. Property
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 39
provisions.content: The inhabitants of the Communal Sections have the right of preemption for the exploitation of the State’s land in the private domain located in their locality.

provisions.chapter: SECTION I. Right to Information
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 40
provisions.content: The State has the obligation to publicize in the oral, written and televised press in the Creole and French languages all laws, orders, decrees, international agreements, treaties, and conventions on everything affecting the national life, except for information concerning national security.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 41
provisions.content: No person of Haitian nationality may be deported or forced to leave the national territory for any reason. No one may be deprived for political reasons of his legal capacity and his nationality.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 41-1
provisions.content: No Haitian needs a visa to leave or return to the country.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 42
provisions.content: No citizen, whether civilian or military, may be denied access to the courts open to him under the Constitution and the laws.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 42-1
provisions.content:
Military personnel accused of the crime of high treason against the country shall be tried in a court of ordinary law.
Establishment of military courts

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 42-2

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 42-3
provisions.content: Cases of conflicts between civilians and military personnel, abuses, violence and crimes perpetrated against a civilian by a member of the military in the performance of his duties are under the jurisdiction of courts for ordinary law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 43
provisions.content: No house search or seizure of papers may take place except under the terms of the law and in the manner prescribed by it.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 44
provisions.content:
Persons detained temporarily awaiting trial must be held separately from those who are serving sentence.
Human dignity

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 44-1
provisions.content:
Prisons must be operated in accordance with standards reflecting respect for human dignity according to the law on this subject.
Principle of no punishment without law

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 45
provisions.content:
No penalty may be established except by law nor applied except in cases that the law determines.
Protection from self-incrimination

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 46
provisions.content: No own may be compelled in cases of crimes, minor offenses, or petty violations to bear witness against himself or his relatives up to the fourth degree of consanguinity or the second degree of affinity.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 47
provisions.content: No one may be compelled to take an oath except in the cases and in the manner provided for by law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 48
provisions.content:
The State shall see to it that a Civil Pension Retirement Fund is established in the public and private sectors. The fund shall receive contributions from employers and employees, in accordance with the criteria and in the manner established by law. The granting of a pension is a right and not a privilege.
Right to privacy, Inalienable rights

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 49
provisions.content:
Freedom and privacy of correspondence and any other forms of communication are inviolable. They may be limited only by a well-founded judicial ruling, according to the guarantees by law.
Jury trials required

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 50
provisions.content:
Under the Constitution and the law, a jury is established in criminal cases for violent crimes and political offenses.
Protection from ex post facto laws

provisions.chapter: SECTION J. Right to Security
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 51
provisions.content: The law may not be made retroactive except in criminal cases when it favors the accused.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 52
provisions.content: Citizenship entails civic duties. Every right is counterbalanced by a corresponding duty.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 52-1

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 52-2
provisions.content: Failure to abide by these provisions shall be punishable by law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.section: TITLE III. Basic Rights and Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 52-3
provisions.content: Compulsory civic service for both sexes is established. The terms thereof shall be set by law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 53
provisions.content: The conditions under which aliens may be admitted to or remain in the country are established by law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 54
provisions.content: Aliens in the territory of the Republic shall enjoy the same protection accorded to Haitians, under the law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 54-1
provisions.content: Aliens enjoy civil, economic and social rights subject to legal provisions on the right to own real property, the practice of a profession, engaging in wholesale trade, serving as a commercial representative, and engaging in import and export operations.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 55
provisions.content: The right to own real property is accorded to aliens resident in Haiti for the needs of their sojourn in the country.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 55-1
provisions.content: However, aliens residing in Haiti may not own more than one dwelling in the name Arrondissement. They may in no case engage in the business of renting real estate. However, foreign companies engaged in real estate promotion shall receive the benefits of a special status regulated by law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 55-2
provisions.content: The right to own real property shall be accorded also to aliens residing in Haiti and to foreign companies for the needs of their agricultural, commercial, industrial, religious, humanitarian or educational enterprises, within the limits and under the conditions prescribed by law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 55-3
provisions.content: No alien may be the owner of a building bounded by the Haitian land order.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 55-4
provisions.content: The right terminates five(5) years after an alien ceases to reside in the country or the operation of this companies have terminates, pursuant to the law establishing regulations to be followed for the transmission and liquidation of property owned by aliens.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 55-5
provisions.content: Violators of the above provisions and their accomplices shall be punished as provided for in the law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 56
provisions.content:
An alien may be expelled from the territory of the Republic if he becomes involved in the political life of the country, or in cases determined by law.
Protection of stateless persons

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 57
provisions.content: The right to asylum for political refugees is recognized.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 58

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 59

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 59-1
provisions.content:
The three (3) powers constitute the essential foundation of the organization of the State, which is civil.
Claim of executive independence, Judicial independence

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 60
provisions.content: Each power is independent of the other two (2) in the powers it exercises separately.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 60-1
provisions.content: None of them may, for any reason, delegate their powers in all or in part, nor go beyond the bounds set for them by the Constitution and by law.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER III. Duties of the Citizen
provisions.title: Article 60-2
provisions.content: Each of the three (3) powers is entirely responsible for its own acts.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. Territorial Divisions and Decentralization
provisions.title: Article 61
provisions.content: The territorial divisions are the Communal Sections, the Communes and the Departments.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER I. Territorial Divisions and Decentralization
provisions.title: Article 61-1
provisions.content:
The law may create any other territorial division.
Municipal government

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Communal Sections
provisions.title: Article 62
provisions.content: The Communal Section is the smallest administrative territorial entity of the Republic.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Communal Sections
provisions.title: Article 63
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Claim of universal suffrage
The administration of each communal section is assured by a council of three (3) members elected by universal suffrage for a term of four (4) years. They are indefinitely re-eligible. Its mode of organization and of functioning is regulated by the law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Communal Sections
provisions.title: Article 63-1
provisions.content:
The Administrative Council of the Communal Section is assisted in its work by an Assembly of the Communal Section.
Right to culture

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Communal Sections
provisions.title: Article 64
provisions.content: The state is obligated to establish for each Communal Section the structures required for social, economic, civic and cultural training of its population.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. Communal Sections
provisions.title: Article 65
provisions.content:
Members of the Administrative Council of the Communal Section must:
be Haitians and be at least twenty-five (25) years of age;
have resided in the Communal Section for two (2) years before the elections and continue to reside there:
enjoy civil and political rights and never been sentenced to death, personal restraint or penal servitude or the loss of civil rights.
Municipal government

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 66
provisions.content: Communes have administrative and financial autonomy. Each Commune of the Republic is administered by a Council, known as the Municipal Council, of three (3) members elected by universal suffrage.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 66-1
provisions.content: The President of the council is assisted in its work by a Municipal Assembly composed among others, of a representative of each of its Communal sections.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 67
provisions.content: The Municipal Council is assisted in its work by a Municipal Assembly composed, among others, of a representative of each of its Communal Sections.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 68
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The mandate of the Municipal Council is of four (4) years and its members are indefinitely re-eligible.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 69
provisions.content: The mode of organization and operation of the Commune and the Municipal Council are regulated by law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 70

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 71
provisions.content: Each Municipal Council is assisted at its request by a Technical Council furnished by the Central Government.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 72
provisions.content:
The Municipal Council may be dissolved for negligence, embezzlement, or maladministration, legally determined by a court of competent jurisdiction.
If it is dissolved, the Departmental Council shall immediately fill the vacancy and call upon the Permanent Electoral Council to elect, in sixty (60) days starting from the date the Council is dissolved, a new Council and shall manage the affairs of the Commune for the remainder of the term. This procedure also applies to vacancies occurring for any other reason.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 73
provisions.content: The Municipal Council manages its resources for the exclusive benefit of the Municipality and renders its accounts to the Municipal Assembly which in turn reports to the Departmental Council.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. Communes
provisions.title: Article 74
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The Municipal Council has the privilege of seeing to the management of the land assets of private domain of the State situated within the limits of the Commune by the competent services conforming to the law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. Arrondissements
provisions.title: Article 75
provisions.content:
The Arrondissement is an administrative division that may comprise several Communes. Its organization and operations are governed by law.
Subsidiary unit government

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 76
provisions.content: The Department is the largest territorial division. It comprises the Arrondissements.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 77
provisions.content: The Department has legal personality and is autonomous.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 78
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Each Department is administered by a council of three (3) members elected for four (4) years by the Departmental Assembly.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 79

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 80
provisions.content: The departmental Council is assisted in its work by a Departmental Assembly made up of: One (1) representative from each Municipal Assembly.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 80-1

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 81
provisions.content: The Departmental Council draws up the Department’s development plan in cooperation with the Central Government.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 82
provisions.content: The organization and operations of the Departmental Council and the Departmental Assembly are regulated by law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 83
provisions.content: The Departmental Council manages its financial resources for the exclusive benefit of the Department and renders its accounts to the Departmental Assembly, which in turn reports to the Central Government.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Departments
provisions.title: Article 84
provisions.content:
The Departmental Council may be dissolved in the event of embezzlement or maladministration legally determined by a court of competent jurisdiction.
If it is dissolved, the Central Government appoints a Provisional Commission and calls upon the Permanent Electoral Council to elect a new Council for the remainder of the term within sixty (60) days of the dissolution.

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Delegates and Vice Delegates
provisions.title: Article 85
provisions.content: In each Departmental Capital, the Executive Power appoints a Representative, who bears the title of Delegate. A Vice Delegate placed under the authority of the Delegate is also appointed in each Arrondissement Capital.

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Delegates and Vice Delegates
provisions.title: Article 86
provisions.content:
Delegates and Vice Delegates ensure coordination and control of public services and exercise no repressive police function.
Other duties of delegates and Vice Delegates are determined by law.
Advisory bodies to the head of state

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Interdepartmental Council
provisions.title: Article 87
provisions.content: The Executive is assisted by an Interdepartmental Council, the members of which are designated by the Departmental Assemblies on the basis of one (1) per Department.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Interdepartmental Council
provisions.title: Article 87-1
provisions.content: This Representative chosen from among the members of the Departmental Assemblies serves as liaison between the Department and the Executive Power.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Interdepartmental Council
provisions.title: Article 87-2
provisions.content: The interdepartmental Council, in concert with the executive, studies and plans projects for decentralization and development of the country from the social, economic, commercial, agricultural and industrial standpoint.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Interdepartmental Council
provisions.title: Article 87-3
provisions.content: It attends working meetings of the Council of Ministers, when they discuss subjects mentioned in the preceding paragraph and has the right to vote.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Interdepartmental Council
provisions.title: Article 87-4
provisions.content: Decentralization must be accompanied by deconcentration of public services with delegation of power and industrial decompartmentalization for the benefit of the departments.

provisions.chapter: SECTION F. Interdepartmental Council
provisions.title: Article 87-5
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The law determines the organization and the functioning of the Interdepartmental Council.

provisions.chapter: CHAPTER II. The Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 88
provisions.content: Legislative power shall be vested in two (2) representative Chambers. One (1) Chamber of Deputies and one (1) Senate, comprising the Legislature or Parliament.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 89
provisions.content:
The Chamber of Deputies is a body composed of members elected by direct suffrage by the citizens and is responsible for exercising, on their behalf and in concert with the Senate, the functions of the legislative power.
Size of first chamber

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 90
provisions.content:
Each Municipal Authority comprises an electoral district and elects one (1) Deputy.
The law sets up to three (3) the number of Deputies at the level of large built-up areas.
Pending application of the above subparagraphs, the number of Deputies may not be fewer than seventy (70).

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 90-1
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
First chamber selection, Scheduling of elections
The election of the Deputy takes place on the last Sunday of October of the fourth year of his mandate. He is elected with the absolute majority of the suffrage expressed in the electoral assemblies through of the valid votes, in accordance with the electoral law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 90-2
provisions.content:
[Inserted by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
First chamber selection
On the occasion of the elections, the candidate to the deputation who is the most favored at the first round not having obtained the absolute majority is declared the victor in the case where his total, in relation to his immediate pursuer, is equal or superior to twenty-five percent (25%).

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 91

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 92
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Term length for first chamber
The Deputies are elected for four (4) years and are indefinitely re-eligible.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 92-1
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
They enter into their functions the second Monday of January which follows their elections and sit in two (2) annual sessions. The duration of their mandate forms one legislature.
In the case where the elections cannot be determined before the second Monday of January, the deputies elected enter into their functions immediately after the validation of the ballot, and their mandate of four (4) years is considered to have commenced on the second Monday of January of the year of the entry into their functions.
Length of legislative sessions

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 92-2
provisions.content: The first session runs from the second Monday of January to the second Monday of May; the second session, from the second Monday of June to the second Monday of September.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 92-3
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The renewal of the Chamber of Deputies is made completely every four (4) years.

provisions.chapter: SECTION A. The Chamber of Deputies
provisions.title: Article 93
provisions.content: Beside the duties conferred upon it by the Constitution as a branch of the Legislature, the Chamber of Deputies has the duty of arraigning the Chief of State, the Prime Minister, the Ministers and the Secretaries of State before the High Court of Justice, by a majority of two-thirds (2/3) of this members. The other powers of the Chamber of Deputies are assigned by the Constitution and by law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 94
provisions.content:
The Senate is a body composed of members elected by direct suffrage of the citizens and charged with exercising on their behalf, in concert with the Chamber of Deputies, the duties of the Legislative Power.
Second chamber selection, Size of second chamber

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 94-1
provisions.content:
The number of Senators is set at three (3) per Department.
Second chamber selection, Claim of universal suffrage

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 94-2
provisions.content: A Senator of Republic is elected by universal suffrage by an absolute majority of votes in the Primary Assemblies held in the geographic Departments, under the terms prescribed by the Electoral Law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 94-3
provisions.content:
[Inserted by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Second chamber selection
On the occasion of the elections, the candidate to the Senate who is the most favored at the first round not having obtained the absolute majority is declared the victor in the case where his total, in relation to his immediate pursuer, is equal or superior to twenty-five percent (25%).

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 95
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
Term length of second chamber
The senators are elected for six (6) years and are indefinitely re-eligible. They enter into their functions on the second Monday of January which follows their elections.
In the case where the elections cannot be determined before the second Monday of January, the senators elected enter into their functions immediately after the validation of the ballot, and their mandate of six (6) years is considered to have commenced on the second Monday of January of the year of the entry into their functions.
Length of legislative sessions

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 95-1
provisions.content:
The Senate is permanently session.
Extraordinary legislative sessions

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 95-2
provisions.content:
The Senate may however adjourn, but not during the Legislative Section. When it adjourns, it leaves a permanent, committee charged with handling current business. The committee may not make any decisions, except to convene the Senate.
In emergencies, the Executive may also convene the Senate before the end of the adjournment period.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 95-3
provisions.content: One-third (1/3) of the Senate is replaced every two (2) years.

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 96

provisions.chapter: SECTION B. The Senate
provisions.title: Article 97

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 98
provisions.content: The meeting in a single Assembly of the two (2) branches of the Legislature constitutes the National Assembly.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 98-1
provisions.content: The National Assembly meets to open and close each session and in all cases provided for by the Constitution.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 98-2
provisions.content: The powers of the National Assembly are limited and may not be extended to matters other than those especially assigned to it by the Constitution.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 98-3

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 99
provisions.content: The National Assembly is presided over by the President of the Senate, assisted by the President of the Chamber of Deputies acting as Vice President. The Secretaries of the Senate and the Chamber of Deputies are the Secretaries of the National Assembly.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 99-1
provisions.content: In the event the President of the Senate is unable to discharge his duties, the National Assembly shall be presided over by the President of the Chamber of Deputies, and the Vice President of the Senate shall then become Vice President of the National Assembly.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 99-2
provisions.content:
In the event the two (2) Presidents are unable to discharge their duties, the two (2) Vice-Presidents shall replace them, respectively.
Public or private sessions

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 100
provisions.content:
Sessions of the National Assembly are public. However, they may be held in closed session at the request of five (5) members, and the resumption of public sessions shall then be decided by an absolute majority.
Extraordinary legislative sessions

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 101
provisions.content: In emergencies, when the Legislature is not in session, the Executive Power may call a special session of the National Assembly.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 102
provisions.content: The National Assembly may not meet or take decisions and pass resolutions without a majority of each of the two (2) Chambers being present.

provisions.chapter: SECTION C. The National Assembly
provisions.title: Article 103
provisions.content: The Legislature has its seat in Port-au-Prince. However, depending on the circumstances, this seat may be transferred elsewhere to the same place and at the same time as that of the Executive Power.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 104
provisions.content:
A session of the Legislature dates from the opening of the two (2) Chambers meeting as the National Assembly.
Extraordinary legislative sessions

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 105
provisions.content: In the interval between regular sessions and in emergencies, the President of the Republic may call a special session of the Legislature.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 106
provisions.content: The Chief of the Executive Power reports on that measure by a message.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 107
provisions.content: In the event the Legislature is convened in special session, it may not decide on any matter other than that for which it was called.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 107-1
provisions.content: However, any Senator or Deputy may introduce a matter of general interest in an Assembly of which he is a member.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 108
provisions.content:
Each Chamber checks and validates the credentials of its members and is the final judge of any disputes that may arise in this regard.
Oaths to abide by constitution

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 109
provisions.content:
The members of each Chamber shall take the following oath:
“I swear to discharge my duties, to maintain and safeguard the rights of the people, and to be faithful to the Constitution”.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 110
provisions.content: Meetings of the two (2) Chambers are public. Each Chamber may meet in closed session at the request of five (5) members, and the decision to resume public meetings shall then be taken by a majority vote.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111
provisions.content:
The Legislature takes the laws on all matters of public interest.
Initiation of general legislation

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-1
provisions.content:
Laws may be initiated by each of the two (2) Chambers as well as by the Executive Power.
Division of labor between chambers, Budget bills, Tax bills, First chamber reserved policy areas, Spending bills

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-2
provisions.content:
However, only the Executive Power may initiate budget laws, laws concerning the assessment, percentage and manner of collecting taxes and contributions, and laws designed to generate revenues or to increase revenues and expenditures of the Government, Bills introduced on these matters must be voted on first by the Chamber of Deputies.
Budget bills, Spending bills, Legislative committees, Tax bills

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-3
provisions.content:
In the event of disagreement between the two (2) Chambers regarding the laws mentioned in the preceding paragraph, each Chamber shall appoint, by voting on a list of an equal number of members, a parliamentary committee that will make a final decision on the disagreement.
Legislative committees

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-4
provisions.content: If a disagreement occurs with regard to any other law, a decision on it will be postponed until the following session. If, at that session, and even in the case of replacement of the Chambers no agreement is reached on the law when it is introduced again, each Chamber shall appoint, by taking a vote on a list of an equal number of members, a parliamentary committee to decide on the final text that will be submitted to the two (2) Assemblies, beginning with the one that originally voted on the law. If these additional deliberations produce no result, the Bill or proposed law will be withdrawn.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-5
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-6
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-7
provisions.content: [Abrogated by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 111-8
provisions.content: In no case may the Chamber of Deputies or the Senate be dissolved or adjourned, nor shall the terms of their members be extended.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 112
provisions.content: Each Chamber shall, in accordance with its regulations appoint its staff, establish discipline for them and determine the manner in which they shall perform their duties.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 112-1
provisions.content:
Each Chamber may impose on its members for reprehensible conduct, by a two thirds (2/3) majority vote, disciplinary penalties, except for expulsion.
Removal of individual legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 113
provisions.content:
Any member of the Legislature shall be disqualified as a Deputy or Senator, if, during his term, he has received a final sentence by a court of regular law, which renders him ineligible to serve.
Immunity of legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 114
provisions.content:
Members of the Legislature are inviolable form the day the take oath up to the expiration of their term, subject to the provisions of Article 115 below.
Immunity of legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 114-1
provisions.content:
They may at no time be prosecuted or attacked for the opinions and votes cast by them in the discharge of their duties.
Immunity of legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 114-2
provisions.content:
No member of the Legislature shall be subject to civil imprisonment during his term of office.
Immunity of legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 115
provisions.content:
No member of the Legislature may during his term be arrested under ordinary law for a crime, a minor offense or a petty violation, except by authorization of the Chamber of which he is a member, unless he is apprehended in the act of committing an offense punishable by death, personal restraint or penal servitude or the loss of civil rights. In that case, the matter is referred to the Chamber of Deputies or the Senate without delay of the Legislature is in session, and if not, it shall be taken up the next regular or special session.
Quorum for legislative sessions

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 116
provisions.content: Neither of the two (2) Chambers may sit nor take action without the presence of a majority of its members.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 117
provisions.content:
All acts of the Legislature must be approved by a majority of the members present, unless otherwise stipulated in this Constitution.
Legislative oversight of the executive

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 118
provisions.content: Each Chamber has the right to investigate matters brought before it.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 119
provisions.content: All bills must be voted on article by article.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 119-1
provisions.content:
[Inserted by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The Executive Power can solicit the benefit of urgency on the vote of a bill of law.
In the case where the solicited benefit of urgency is obtained, the bill of law is voted article by article, all other matters ceasing.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 120
provisions.content: Each Chamber has the right to amend and to divide articles and amendments proposed. Amendments voted on by one Chamber may be part of a bill only after it has been voted on by the other Chamber in the same form and in identical terms. No bill shall become a law until it has been voted on in the same form by the two (2) Chambers.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 120-1
provisions.content:
Any bill may be withdrawn from discussion so long as it has not been finally voted upon.
Approval or veto of general legislation

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 121
provisions.content: Any bill passed by the Legislature shall be immediately forwarded to the President of the Republic, who, before promulgation it, has the right to make objections to it in all or in part.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 121-1
provisions.content: In such cases, the President of the Republic send back the bill with his objections to the Chamber where it was originally passed, If the bill is amended by that house, it is sent to the other Chambers with the objections.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 121-2
provisions.content:
If the bill thus amended is voted on by the second Chamber, it will be sent back to the President of the Republic for promulgation.
Veto override procedure

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 121-3
provisions.content:
If the objection are rejected by the Chamber that originally passed the bill, it shall be returned to the other Chamber with the objections.
Veto override procedure

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 121-4
provisions.content:
If the second Chamber also votes to reject it, the bill is sent back to the President of the Republic, who must then promulgate it.
Secrecy of legislative votes, Veto override procedure

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 121-5
provisions.content:
Rejection of the objection is voted on by either Chamber by the majority stipulated in Article 117. In such cases, the votes of each Chamber shall be taken by secret ballot.
Approval or veto of general legislation

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 121-6
provisions.content:
If in either Chamber the Majority stipulated in the preceding paragraph is not obtained for the rejection, the objections are accepted.
Approval or veto of general legislation

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 122
provisions.content:
The right of objection must be exercised within eight (8) full days starting with the date of the receipt of the bill by the President of the Republic.
Approval or veto of general legislation

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 123
provisions.content: If within the prescribed deadline, the President of the Republic has made expiration, the bill must be promulgated unless the session of the Legislature has ended before exploration of the deadline, in which case, the bill is deferred. At the opening of the following session, the bill thus deferred is sent to the President of the Republic to exercise his right of objection.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 124
provisions.content: A bill rejected by one of the two (2) Chambers may not be introduced again in the same session.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 125
provisions.content: Bills and other acts of the Legislature and the National Assembly shall enter into force with their promulgation and their publication in the Official Gazette (Journal Officiel) of the Republic.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 125-1
provisions.content: Bills shall be numbered and included in the printed and numbered bulletin entitled BULLETIN OF LAWS AND ACTS.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 126
provisions.content: The bill is dated on the day of it final adoption by the two (2) Chambers.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 127
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
No one may present petitions in person to the tribune of the Legislative Power. Any petition addressed to the Legislative Power must give rise to a regulatory procedure that permits deciding on its object.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 128
provisions.content: Only the Legislature Power has the authority to interpret laws, which it does by passing a law.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 129
provisions.content:
Each member of the Legislature receives a monthly stipend from the time he takes oath.
Outside professions of legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 129-1
provisions.content:
Service as a member of the Legislature is incompatible with any other duty remunerated by the State, except that of teacher.
Legislative oversight of the executive

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 129-2
provisions.content:
Every member of the two (2) Chambers has the right to question and interpellate a member of the Government or the entire Government on events and acts of the Administration.
Legislative oversight of the executive, Head of government removal

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 129-3
provisions.content:
As interpellation request must be seconded by five (5) members of the body concerned. It becomes a vote of confidence or of censure when passed by a majority of that body.
Head of government removal, Legislative oversight of the executive

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 129-4
provisions.content:
When the interpellation request ends in a votes of censure on a question concerning a Government program or declaration of general policy, the Prime Minister must submit his Government’s resignation to the President of the Republic.
Head of government removal

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 129-5
provisions.content:
The president must accept that resignation and appoint new Prime Minister, pursuant to the provisions of this Constitution.
Head of government removal, Limits on removing head of government

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 129-6
provisions.content:
[Amended by the Constitutional Law of 9 May 2011 / 19 June 2012]
The Legislative Power may not take, as it concerns the Prime Minister, more than one vote of censure per year.
Legislative oversight of the executive
Any Prime Minister having obtained a vote of confidence may only be interpellated in a time of six (6) months after this vote of confidence.
The defeat of a motion of censure, submitted to the vote of the two Chambers, as it concerns the Prime Minister, is equivalent to a vote of confidence.
Replacement of legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 130
provisions.content:
In the case of the death, resignation, disqualification, judicial interdiction, or acceptance of a duty incompatible with that of a member of the Legislature, the Deputy or Senator shall be replaced in his Electoral District for only the remainder of his term by a by-election called by the Primary Electoral Assembly to be conducted by the Permanent Electoral Council in the month he vacancy occurs.
Replacement of legislators

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 130-1
provisions.content: The election shall take place within thirty (30) days after convocation of the Primary Assembly, pursuant to the Constitution.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 130-2
provisions.content: The same procedure shall apply in the absence of an election or in the event that elections are declared null and void by the Permanent Electoral Council in one or more Electoral Districts.

provisions.chapter: SECTION D. Exercise of Legislative Power
provisions.title: Article 130-3
provisions.content: However, if the vacancy occurs during the last regular session of the Legislature or after that session, a by-election may not be held.

provisions.chapter: SECTION E. Incompatibilities
provisions.title: Article 131
